exemplar.title: M25 #1: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #1: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

strengths: The essay maintains a sustained focus on the prescribed title and consistently weaves together History and Human Sciences.
Key terms (âethical obligation,â âcontradictory evidenceâ) are clearly defined and applied, framing the analysis with precision.
Arguments are coherent and effectively supported by specific, well-chosen examples (Battle of Kadesh, psychology replication crisis).
Integration of Kantâs categorical imperative and statistical data on replication success rates demonstrates conceptual understanding across AOKs.
There is clear awareness and evaluation of alternative perspectives, including counter-claims about propaganda and methodological variance.
weaknesses: Depth of evaluation is at times surface-level: implications for public trust and policy are not fully explored.
Some transitions are abrupt and structural clarity suffers (e.g., psychology section placement, lack of preview of comparative structure).
The conclusion lacks a definitive synthesis tying the comparative analysis back to the prescribed title, reducing overall resolution.
Opportunities for deeper cross-AOK comparison and discussion of ways of knowing are sometimes missed.
Reliance on a non-academic source for the definition of ethical obligation limits academic rigor.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay meets the descriptors for the top of the 7â8 band: it sustains focus on the title, links effectively to both History and Human Sciences, and develops clear, coherent arguments supported by pertinent examples. It demonstrates awareness and some evaluation of different viewpoints. The few weaknessesâsurface-level implication analysis, structural and coherence lapses, and a concluding section that could tie points together more definitivelyâprevent it from entering the 9â10 band. Allocating 8 marks reflects its strong performance overall while acknowledging areas for deeper critical exploration and structural refinement.

annotations.comment: Linking reproducibility challenges to ethical obligations parallels the historical case study, demonstrating effective cross-AOK linkage.
The Confucius quotation is evocative but remains disconnected from the subsequent analysis; an explicit link to the core argument would strengthen coherence.
Discussing source intentions and propaganda deepens the ethical complexity, demonstrating nuanced understanding of historical evidence.
This section lacks explicit contrast with human sciences, missing an opportunity for cross-AOK comparison.
Clear statistical data on replication success rates concretely exemplifies the replication crisis in psychology.
Integration of Kantâs categorical imperative neatly grounds the ethical dimension in historical methodology, showing conceptual understanding.
Effective restatement of the prescribed title provides a clear focus and signals to the reader how the essay will engage with the question.
Definitions of âethical obligationâ and âcontradictoryâ frame the analysis clearly and help establish a precise scope for discussion.
Reliance on Empire State University as the source for the definition of ethical obligation limits academic rigor; consider a peer-reviewed or disciplinary definition.
The thesis statement outlines the aim but does not explicitly preview the comparative structure for history and human sciences, reducing structural clarity.
The introduction does not address how ways of knowing influence engagement with evidence, missing an opportunity to deepen the TOK perspective.
The Battle of Kadesh example is well chosen to illustrate how contradictory primary sources shape historical knowledge claims.
Citation of Trevor R. Bryce (2005) demonstrates engagement with reputable historiographical scholarship to support the stalemate interpretation.
The narrative of Kadesh remains largely descriptive; deeper critique of source provenance and historiographical debate would enhance analysis.
Transition into potential selective focus (âIf a historian were to selectively focusâ¦â) feels abrupt; smoother linking sentences would improve coherence.
The fragment on psychology appears here out of context, disrupting the flow; restructure to ensure clear section separation.
Durantâs quotation effectively highlights the inherent limitations of historical knowledge and shows strong historiographical self-awareness.
Presentation of conflicting figures (100 studies vs. 28 replications) is confusing; clarify sample sizes and methodology for precision.
The discussion omits consideration of broader implications (e.g., impact on public trust or policy), limiting the TOK depth of analysis.
The argument that ignoring propagandistic evidence can be ethical needs more critical evaluation of consequences for historical accuracy.
The claim that all contradictory evidence is equally legitimate oversimplifies nuance; distinguishing types of evidence would refine the argument.
Acknowledgement of contextual and methodological variations adds important nuance to the human sciences discussion.
Assertion that human scientists hold no ethical responsibility under unattainable conditions requires supporting evidence or illustrative counterexamples.
Final question on universality versus context-dependence of ethical obligations demonstrates sophisticated TOK reflection.
Conclusion lacks a definitive stance or synthesis that ties the comparative analysis back to the prescribed title, reducing resolution.

described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
